
The Ideal Grounded Draft (The Final Framework Applied)
She wiped the alchemical chalk from her fingers onto her sleeve. White dust against Dalaran blue.

Kel'Thuzad's head turned before hers did. Small motion. Attention lifting.

The blood-wards answered a beat later.

The flagstone took the disturbance first. A kinetic pulse. Then another, farther up the crypt spine. Scholomance's old protections took the descent in order. No lock-picking. No clumsy dispel magic. Each runic threshold answered and handed the motion downward.

Kel'Thuzad stood. "Interesting," he said.

Jaina was already on her feet.

The next ripple came clearer. Stone. Bone. Stone again. Whoever was diving knew how to clear a kill-zone and kept their momentum through the choke.

Kel'Thuzad looked not at the iron door but at her. She understood why a moment later. The recognition had spiked her aura before she could master it.

"Someone expected?" he asked.

Jaina did not answer.

Another kinetic pulse. Closer now. The glyphs ruptured one after another to leather boots driving over them without breaking stride. A pause at the ossuary gate. Angle checked. Then a sprint again, faster than before.

Kel'Thuzad's eyes narrowed. "You know our visitor."

She did. She knew that cadence. Light armor crossing flagstones without drag, every blind corner cleared on the draw. She knew what kind of hunter had breached the crypt.

---
The main southern approach was a wall of iron and shouting men.

Arthas’s column had arrived. The sound of it was deafening—the rhythmic beat of hooves, the clank of plate armor, the barking of orders. Falric was at the archway, his face caked in road grit.

"Where is he?" Jaina asked, pushing through the guards.

"Main gate. With Lord Uther."

They found them under the heavy stone arch of the city’s formal entrance. Arthas was still mounted, his hand white-knuckled on the grip of his hammer. Uther stood beside him, looking like a monument of tired gold.

Arthas saw her and dismounted before his groom could even grab the reins. He strode through the mud, his eyes searching hers with a desperate, terrifying intensity.

"Well?" he demanded. "Did you stop it?"

Jaina looked at him. She looked at the flour dust on her sleeves and the blood on Sylvanas’s cloak.

"I spoiled two warehouses," she said.

Arthas let out a breath, a ghost of a smile touching his lips. "Then we have a chance. If we move the priests in now—"

"Listen to me, Arthas." Jaina’s voice cracked. "The warehouses were half-empty when we got there. The morning loads are gone. The bread is in the markets. It’s in the church kitchens. People are already eating it on the streets."

The smile vanished. Arthas’s face went very still. "How much of the city?"

"I don't know," Jaina said. "A lot of it. Enough."

"Give me a real answer, Jaina!"

"There isn't one!" she snapped back. "I can ruin wheat in a sack, Arthas. I can't reach into a thousand stomachs and pull it back out."

Silence fell over the officers. Uther closed his eyes, his head bowing slightly.

Arthas took a step closer to her. He smelled of horse and ozone. "Can the city be saved?"

Jaina looked past him, into the heart of Stratholme. She saw the smoke of the bakeries and heard the distant, oblivious laughter of the crowds.

"No," she said. "Not like this."

Arthas didn't blink. His eyes went cold—a flat, hard blue that she didn't recognize. He turned away from her, looking at the city walls as if he were already measuring them for a coffin.
